jokes.text: I told you to keep away from my daughter. No, Daddy, you'll hurt him. I love him. Goodbye, Norman! Goodbye!
jokes.setup: The farmer discovers Newman with his daughter.
jokes.punchline: In one night, Newman has been ejected from a mail truck, ambushed by survivalists (in his telling), eaten mutton, declared himself a Nautilus devotee, and now must flee a farmer defending his daughter's honor — all while his daughter calls out 'Norman!'
jokes.explanation: Perfect callback payoff. The farmer warned him, and Newman — true to form — couldn't help himself. The daughter crying 'Goodbye, Norman!' as Newman flees is both romantic and completely absurd in context. Newman violating the one rule he was given, in a single night, is completely in character. 'Norman!' is an unexpected callback to Jerry's famous name for him.

jokes.text: I'll be inaugurating them this weekend with none other than Ethel Kennedy. A woman whose triumph in the face of tragedy is exceeded only by her proclivity to procreate.
jokes.setup: Peterman receives the golf clubs and announces his plans for them.
jokes.punchline: Peterman's description of Ethel Kennedy: 'whose triumph in the face of tragedy is exceeded only by her proclivity to procreate' — an oddly sexualized, condescendingly phrased tribute.
jokes.explanation: One of Peterman's finest lines. 'Proclivity to procreate' as a defining characteristic of Ethel Kennedy is a perfectly Peterman way to describe having many children — simultaneously respectful and bizarrely reductive. The word 'proclivity' gives it a clinical-yet-admiring quality. Only Peterman speaks this way. Highly quotable, exceptional character integration.

jokes.text: Peterman examines the clubs and notices damage. Elaine produces 'the letter of authenticity' from the side pocket — but the clubs have clearly been used as weapons.
jokes.setup: Peterman has his $20,000 JFK clubs at last
jokes.punchline: The clubs that have been used in a highway golf-club fight are now being certified with a letter of authenticity as presidential memorabilia — the contrast between their recent adventure and their official status is the joke
jokes.explanation: The letter of authenticity for clubs that were just thrown at a mail truck in Ohio is a nice ironic beat. The setup pays off the entire episode's arc cleanly.

jokes.text: Elaine. I never knew Kennedy had such a temper. Oh, yeah. The only thing worse was his slice.
jokes.setup: Peterman notices the damaged golf clubs — Kennedy apparently had a temper based on the damage.
jokes.punchline: 'The only thing worse was his slice.' Elaine deflects from revealing the clubs were damaged in a car chase by attributing the damage to JFK's bad golf game.
jokes.explanation: The perfect episode button. The damage to the clubs (from a mechanic/car chase) is reattributed to JFK's bad temper and his 'slice' — and Peterman entirely accepts this as historically plausible. 'The only thing worse was his slice' is an ideal final line: economical, historically absurd, completely in character for Elaine to improvise, and lands with satisfying punch. Among the episode's best jokes — a great closer.

jokes.text: Peterman: 'I never knew Kennedy had such a temper.' / Elaine: 'Oh, yeah. The only thing worse was his slice.'
jokes.setup: Peterman notices the clubs are damaged (from Tony throwing them) and attributes this to JFK's temper
jokes.punchline: 'The only thing worse was his slice' — the double meaning (golf slice = errant shot; metaphorical slice = violent tendency) perfectly closes the episode
jokes.explanation: The episode's closing joke earns its position. 'The only thing worse was his slice' functions perfectly as both a golf observation and an implication about JFK's temperament, using the clubs' battered state (from Tony's attack) as evidence. The word 'slice' carries the entire payload. Near-perfect structure, economy, and character integration — Elaine's deadpan knowingness is perfect. A top-tier closing line. Note this is a re-score of the same joke as #27; the closing delivery with full episode context makes this the canonical entry.

tmdb_overview: George gets his boss, George Steinbrenner, hooked on calzones, then loses his supplier; Kramer warms to clothing fresh from the dryer.
insight: Mid-tier episode coasts on character quirks without landing memorable punchlines or escalations.
